texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract: Hidden Markov chain, or Markov field, models, with observations in a Euclidean space, play a major role across signal and image processing. The present work provides a statistical framework which can be used to extend these models, along with related, popular algorithms (such as the Baum-Welch algorithm), to the case where the observations lie in a Riemannian manifold. It is motivated by the potential use of hidden Markov chains and fields, with observations in Riemannian manifolds, as models for complex signals and images.
texts.text: Abstract: Hidden Markov chain, or Markov field, models, with observations in a Euclidean space, play a major role across signal and image processing. The present work provides a statistical framework which can be used to extend these models, along with related, popular algorithms (such as the Baum-Welch algorithm), to the case where the observations lie in a Riemannian manifold. It is motivated by the potential use of hidden Markov chains and fields, with observations in Riemannian manifolds, as models for complex signals and images.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Keywords: Riemannian manifold, hidden Markov model, Markov field, EM algorithm
texts.text: Keywords: Riemannian manifold, hidden Markov model, Markov field, EM algorithm

texts.orig: 1. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: 1. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The present work is concerned with hidden Markov chain and Markov field models, with observations in Riemannian manifolds.
texts.text: The present work is concerned with hidden Markov chain and Markov field models, with observations in Riemannian manifolds.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It introduces a general formulation of hidden Markov chain models, whose observations lie in a Riemannian manifold, and derives an expectation-maximisation algorithm, to estimate the parameters of these models - Sections 2 and 3, respectively.
texts.text: It introduces a general formulation of hidden Markov chain models, whose observations lie in a Riemannian manifold, and derives an expectation-maximisation algorithm, to estimate the parameters of these models - Sections 2 and 3, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It also describes a general hidden Markov field model, with observations in a Riemannian manifold, and discusses briefly the estimation of this model, using the expectationmaximisation approach - Section 5.
texts.text: It also describes a general hidden Markov field model, with observations in a Riemannian manifold, and discusses briefly the estimation of this model, using the expectationmaximisation approach - Section 5.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hidden Markov chain and field models have been highly influential in signal and image analysis[1][2]. For example, the Baum-Welch algorithm, for estimating hidden Markov chain models, is a staple of applications such as speech recognition and protein sequencing [3][4]. Also, hidden Markov fields are central in several approaches to image restoration and segmentation [5].
texts.text: Hidden Markov chain and field models have been highly influential in signal and image analysis[1][2]. For example, the Baum-Welch algorithm, for estimating hidden Markov chain models, is a staple of applications such as speech recognition and protein sequencing [3][4]. Also, hidden Markov fields are central in several approaches to image restoration and segmentation [5].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While quite extensive, existing literature is almost entirely focused on hidden Markov chain and field models with observations in a Euclidean space. The present work aims to provide a statistical framework, which may be used to extend current algorithms, dealing with observations in a Euclidean space, to observations in more general Riemannian manifolds.
texts.text: While quite extensive, existing literature is almost entirely focused on hidden Markov chain and field models with observations in a Euclidean space. The present work aims to provide a statistical framework, which may be used to extend current algorithms, dealing with observations in a Euclidean space, to observations in more general Riemannian manifolds.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For example, the expectation-maximisation algorithm, derived in Section 3, extends the popular Baum-Welch algorithm to hidden Markov chains with observations in a Riemannian manifold.
texts.text: For example, the expectation-maximisation algorithm, derived in Section 3, extends the popular Baum-Welch algorithm to hidden Markov chains with observations in a Riemannian manifold.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The motivation for considering hidden Markov chains and fields with observations in Riemannian manifolds comes from the important role which data in Riemannian manifolds can play in the study of complex signals and images (or even three-dimensional fields, such as turbulent flows [6]).
texts.text: The motivation for considering hidden Markov chains and fields with observations in Riemannian manifolds comes from the important role which data in Riemannian manifolds can play in the study of complex signals and images (or even three-dimensional fields, such as turbulent flows [6]).

texts.orig: To understand this, consider the case of a non-stationary, multivariate signal, observed over a long time interval. This is typical of a phased-array radar, or brain-computer interface signal [7][8].
texts.text: To understand this, consider the case of a non-stationary, multivariate signal, observed over a long time interval. This is typical of a phased-array radar, or brain-computer interface signal [7][8].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While this signal cannot be entirely described by a single tractable model, it is still possible to 'zoom in' on short time windows, which admit of a complete, tractable description, very often in terms of a covariance matrix (for array radar signals, this has an additional block-Toeplitz structure [7]).
texts.text: While this signal cannot be entirely described by a single tractable model, it is still possible to 'zoom in' on short time windows, which admit of a complete, tractable description, very often in terms of a covariance matrix (for array radar signals, this has an additional block-Toeplitz structure [7]).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Now, the original complex signal appears as a time series of covariance matrices, or other descriptors, one for each short time window, and it seems possible to extract useful information, by studying the statistical distribution of these descriptors.
texts.text: Now, the original complex signal appears as a time series of covariance matrices, or other descriptors, one for each short time window, and it seems possible to extract useful information, by studying the statistical distribution of these descriptors.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This idea can also be applied to a complex image, or three-dimensional field, by zooming in on small connected regions[9][6]. Recently, it has generated significant interest in the problem of estimating an unknown distribution, underlying a population of descriptors ( e.g. , covariance matrices, principal subspaces, histogrammes) [10][11][12].
texts.text: This idea can also be applied to a complex image, or three-dimensional field, by zooming in on small connected regions[9][6]. Recently, it has generated significant interest in the problem of estimating an unknown distribution, underlying a population of descriptors ( e.g. , covariance matrices, principal subspaces, histogrammes) [10][11][12].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mathematically, this problem was framed as the problem of estimating an unknown probability distribution on a Riemannian manifold. Indeed, in several applications, this Riemannian approach has become standard, due to its superior performance ( e.g. , brain-computer interface [8]).
texts.text: Mathematically, this problem was framed as the problem of estimating an unknown probability distribution on a Riemannian manifold. Indeed, in several applications, this Riemannian approach has become standard, due to its superior performance ( e.g. , brain-computer interface [8]).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Existing work on this problem has always started from the assumption that descriptors, obtained from different time windows of a signal (or regions of an image), are sampled independently, all from the same underlying distribution. This assumption obscures the dynamics of the signal (or the spatial dependence structure within the image), potentially leading to the loss of crucial information.
texts.text: Existing work on this problem has always started from the assumption that descriptors, obtained from different time windows of a signal (or regions of an image), are sampled independently, all from the same underlying distribution. This assumption obscures the dynamics of the signal (or the spatial dependence structure within the image), potentially leading to the loss of crucial information.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The statistical framework introduced in the present work captures this information, in the form of a hidden Markov dependence structure, which controls the distribution of the descriptors.
